metadata.name: Titanium
metadata.title: Titanium Settings
metadata.description: When laser cleaning titanium, I've seen it stand out from denser metals like steel because of its lighter weight and strong natural oxide layer that fights corrosion so well. This makes it great for aerospace parts, where you can remove tough contaminants without deep damage, but the layer also means the laser tends to skim the surface gently—start with milder power and slower passes to let heat dissipate evenly and avoid uneven heating. Unlike aluminum, which scatters heat quickly, titanium holds it locally, so building up multiple light passes clears buildup effectively while preserving its durability. Just watch out at the end for any signs of discoloration, and dial back if needed to keep that protective shine intact.

author.name: Ikmanda Roswati

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Titanium oxide layer removal

wavelength.description: Optimal wavelength for Titanium absorption characteristics

spotSize.description: Optimal beam spot diameter for Titanium surface treatment

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient Titanium cleaning process

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Titanium surface cleaning

pulseWidth.description: Optimal pulse duration for Titanium ablation without thermal damage

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Titanium cleaning

passCount.description: Optimal number of passes for thorough Titanium cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for complete Titanium surface coverage

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning systems; starting at 100W balances efficiency and safety for Ti's high reflectivity and melting point (~1668°C). Max avoids substrate damage via thermal accumulation. Source: IPG Photonics specs and studies on Ti alloy cleaning (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective for contamination removal; slower for heavy contaminants (e.g., oxides), faster for light. Recommended 1500 mm/s optimizes fluence without overheating Ti's conductive surface. Source: Industry manuals (CleanLaser) and process optimization in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for Ti cleaning to ablate contaminants while minimizing heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); picosecond/femtosecond for ultra-precision but costlier. 20 ns recommended for low thermal damage. Source: Laser physics reviews in Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on metal ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat buildup on reflective Ti. 50 kHz balances speed and minimal accumulation. Trade-off: >80 kHz may require cooling. Source: Academic lit. e.g., J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. (2017) on pulse overlap effects.

energy_density.description: Fluence below Ti's ablation threshold (~3 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal without substrate etching. Recommended 1.0 J/cm² for industrial prep. Ti's reflectivity (~40% at 1064 nm) requires higher input. Source: Ablation studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2021).

spot_size.description: Smaller for fine detail (e.g., aerospace Ti parts), larger for broad areas. 300 μm recommended for uniform cleaning on Ti without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specs from Laserax and Coherent, aligned with ISO 11553 standards.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination; multi-pass for heavy (e.g., rust/scale on Ti). Recommended 2 passes ensures complete removal with low risk to Ti's integrity. Source: Case studies in industrial laser cleaning (e.g., Proc. SPIE 2022) for metal surfaces.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for Ti to avoid gaps or over-etching while managing heat from conductivity. Higher for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization in J. Clean. Prod. (2019) and manufacturer guidelines (e.g., Adapt Laser).
